skill.name: typescript-advanced-types
skill.description: Guidance for using TypeScript advanced types, including generics, conditional types, mapped types, template literal types, and reusable utility types.
skill.summary: Build stronger TypeScript APIs and utilities with advanced type-system patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell execution, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and network indicators, but the referenced files are Markdown documentation and TypeScript examples. I found no executable scripts, no command execution path, no credential handling, no prompt injection text, and no malicious intent; remaining concerns are documentation-only false positives.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged Markdown backticks and TypeScript code fences as Ruby or shell execution. These lines are documentation examples and resource references, not executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced content is Markdown around TypeScript examples and inline resource paths. The skill contains only SKILL.md and a Markdown playbook, with no executable script file or shell invocation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern Not Present
low_findings.description: High-severity weak cryptography alerts point to descriptive text and form-validation examples. I did not find MD5, SHA1, hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API usage in the referenced files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss TypeScript use cases and simple string validation. There is no evidence of weak cryptographic algorithms or security-sensitive hashing behavior.

low_findings.title: Documentation-Only Network References
low_findings.description: Network indicators are illustrative API-client type examples and external learning links. The API client method has a placeholder implementation and no HTTP client import, fetch call, or data transmission.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API-client section demonstrates type-safe method signatures and returns a placeholder value. The URLs are static references to TypeScript documentation resources, not runtime endpoints.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Terms Are Type Examples
low_findings.description: System reconnaissance alerts point to TypeScript examples for event emitters, API routes, state handling, and best practices. No system inspection commands or host enumeration behavior are present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced snippets are TypeScript teaching examples. I found no commands, filesystem traversal, process inspection, or environment probing.

content.value_statement: Advanced TypeScript types can become hard to design, review, and maintain. This skill gives structured patterns for generics, conditional types, mapped types, and type-safe APIs.
actual_capabilities: Explains generics, constraints, conditional types, mapped types, and template literal types.
Provides reusable patterns for event emitters, API clients, builders, forms, and state models.
Shows type inference techniques using infer, type guards, and assertion functions.
Lists best practices, common pitfalls, and performance considerations for complex types.
Helps adapt advanced type examples into project-specific TypeScript designs.
limitations: It is documentation-only and does not run a TypeScript compiler.
Examples are generic and may require project-specific adaptation.
It does not replace runtime validation or application security review.
Very complex recursive types may still need manual performance testing.

use_cases.title: Design a Typed Library API
use_cases.description: Create reusable generic APIs with clear constraints, inferred return types, and safer public contracts.

use_cases.title: Strengthen Frontend Data Models
use_cases.description: Model forms, events, configuration paths, and UI state with stricter compile-time guarantees.

use_cases.title: Guide a JavaScript Migration
use_cases.description: Introduce advanced TypeScript patterns gradually while avoiding common type-safety and performance pitfalls.

prompt_templates.title: Explain an Advanced Type
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of an unfamiliar TypeScript type pattern.
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain this TypeScript advanced type in plain language. Identify its inputs, output shape, and one safe use case.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Utility Type
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate creation of reusable project types.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a TypeScript utility type for this data model. Include the reasoning, edge cases, and a short type-testing approach.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor API Typing
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced improvement of typed API clients or service layers.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this API typing approach and propose a safer design using generics, conditional types, and inferred responses.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Type Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert review of a complex type system before broad adoption.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this TypeScript type architecture for maintainability, compile-time cost, and type-safety gaps. Recommend specific simplifications.

output_examples.input: I need a type-safe event emitter for user lifecycle events.
output_examples.output: A typed event-map design, listener typing guidance, emit-call validation notes, and edge cases for missing event payload fields.

output_examples.input: My form validation types are hard to maintain.
Review this recursive path type before I ship it.
output_examples.output: A maintainability review covering recursion depth, compile-time performance, naming clarity, and simpler alternatives.

best_practices: Start with simple types, then add conditional or mapped logic only when it removes duplication.
Pair advanced types with type tests so regressions are visible during review.
Document complex utility types with examples of accepted and rejected inputs.
anti_patterns: Using any to bypass problems that should be modeled with unknown, guards, or constraints.
Building deeply recursive types without checking compiler performance on real project inputs.
Encoding runtime validation assumptions only in types without validating external data.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code?
faq.answer: No. It provides Markdown guidance and TypeScript examples only.

faq.question: Can it replace the TypeScript compiler?
faq.answer: No. Use it for design guidance, then verify results with your compiler and type tests.

faq.question: Which TypeScript topics does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers generics, conditional types, mapped types, template literal types, utility types, inference, and type guards.

faq.question: Is it useful for API client design?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a type-safe API client pattern for paths, methods, parameters, bodies, and responses.

faq.question: Can beginners use it?
faq.answer: Yes, but it is most useful after learning basic interfaces, unions, functions, and strict TypeScript settings.

faq.question: What should I check before adopting a complex type?
faq.answer: Check readability, compiler performance, edge cases, and whether runtime validation is still required.
